# AQA GCSE English Literature — Unseen Poetry — 10 Worked Past-Paper Pairs (2017–2025)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section C (Unseen Poetry). Q27.1 single-poem analysis (24 marks, ~30 minutes, ~600 words). Q27.2 method comparison to a second printed unseen poem (8 marks, ~12-15 minutes, ~250 words). AOs assessed: AO1 (interpretation, evidence) + AO2 (writer's methods). AO3 (context) and AO4 (SPaG) are NOT assessed in Section C. AQA prints BOTH poems on the paper; the comparison is METHOD, not theme.

## How this Q-bank works

Unlike the named-anthology Q-banks (Power and Conflict, Love and Relationships, Worlds and Lives), Unseen Poetry has no anthology to predict against — AQA prints any two poems from the wider body of English poetry each year. What CAN be rehearsed is the method. This Q-bank rebuilds the section as ten WORKED PAST-PAPER PAIRS that AQA has actually set (2017 specimen through 2025). Each Q reproduces the real poems AQA printed in that year, then walks through a Grade-9 worked plan for Q27.1 + Q27.2 against those poems. The method is fixed; the poem is variable. Whichever pair AQA prints in 2026, the FLAME-scan, TTECEA+C body shape, and method-comparison phrasing pattern carry across.

Section IDs (Q1–Q10) are chronological — 2017 specimen first, 2025 last. Rehearsal priority is not chronological; rehearse the pairs whose stem theme matches your weakest method-cluster.

## Past-paper pair index

| Q | Year | Q27.1 anchor (poem A) | Q27.2 comparison (poem B) | Stem theme |

|---:|---|---|---|---|

| 1 | 2017 Specimen | I Am Very Bothered (Simon Armitage) | Against Road Building (Sophie Hannah) | Regret / cost of action |

| 2 | June 2018 | On Aging (Maya Angelou) | Jessie Emily Schofield (Judy Williams) | Aging and its effects |

| 3 | June 2019 | The Richest Poor Man in the Valley (Lindsay Macrae) | Nobody (Michael Laskey) | How to live your life |

| 4 | November 2020 | I Am Offering this Poem (Jimmy Santiago Baca) | The sun has burst the sky (Jenny Joseph) | Feelings about love |

| 5 | November 2021 | A London Thoroughfare. 2 A.M. (Amy Lowell) | November night, Edinburgh (Norman MacCaig) | A city at night |

| 6 | June 2022 | Shoulders (Naomi Shihab Nye) | Choices (Tess Gallagher) | Care and responsibility |

| 7 | June 2023 | Scaffolding (Seamus Heaney) | Yours (Daniel Hoffman) | The strength of a relationship |

| 8 | June 2024 | The Washing Never Gets Done (Jaan Kaplinski) | A Day in Autumn (R S Thomas) | Daily life and the natural world |

| 9 | June 2025 | Table (Robert Hull) | Like a Beacon (Grace Nichols) | A meaningful object / connection to home |

| 10 | 2017 Specimen — alternative pair | Poem for My Sister (Liz Lochhead) | I Am Very Bothered (Simon Armitage) | Sibling protection / regret across years |

Pair confidence: Q1–Q9 are confirmed against AQA-published papers or mark schemes. Q10 reuses the 2017 specimen alternate-question pairing teaching resources record from the AQA sample set — included so the student has a tenth worked pair against a different stem.

## FLAME method-first reading — the five-minute exam scan

Students lose marks by rushing to content. The mark scheme rewards close analysis of method. Sophicly's house acronym is FLAME: Form, Language (word choice + figurative), Architecture (structure + voltic turn — the turn in the poem's argument), Music (sound + rhythm), Effect-on-reader. Scan FLAME during the second reading of the poem. Whichever of the five elements is doing the heaviest work in the poem in front of you becomes the spine of the essay. A method-first reading produces topic sentences that argue concept; a content-first reading produces topic sentences that summarise plot. Topic sentences that summarise plot cap at Band 4.

## Pedagogical depth — two non-negotiables

1. Every Unseen poem rewards reading AGAINST its surface meaning — find the GENERATIVE TENSION.

The Grade-9 reading of any Unseen poem identifies the standard reading (the surface theme the stem names) AND the counter-reading the same evidence supports. For a love poem, find the line that COMPLICATES love. For a poem about how to live, find the line where the speaker's confidence is shadowed by limit. For a poem about a city, find the line where the city becomes alienating. Every poem worth setting at GCSE level contains the line that argues against its surface. The dominant theme is what most students will write about; the GENERATIVE TENSION is what carries the essay past the Band 5 line. Method-first analytical work is the discipline that locates the generative tension — usually inside a single image, a single shift in tone, a single grammatical inversion, or the volta (turn in the poem's argument). The Grade-9 reading does not catalogue evidence FOR the surface theme; it identifies the moment the surface theme is COMPLICATED, then traces the method by which the complication is achieved. Every BP3 (closing-of-poem paragraph), every Q27.1 conclusion, every Q27.2 method-differential should land on the generative tension the poem stages. House move: "The poem presents [surface theme], yet [specific line / image / structural pivot] complicates the surface: [the counter-reading]. The complication is the poem's deepest move."

2. Q27.2's 8 marks reward METHOD comparison, not CONTENT comparison.

This is the most-repeated examiner remark across AQA examiner reports for Section C. Students lose marks by writing "Both poems present love" — that is content comparison, and it is not what Q27.2 asks. Students gain marks by writing "Poem A presents love through accumulating natural-image personification, while Poem B presents love through a sequence of accumulating practical objects. The differential reveals that Poem A stages love as cosmic event and Poem B stages love as continuing material gift." That is method comparison, and it is what Q27.2 asks. Sophicly's house phrasing pattern: "Poem A presents [theme] through [method A], while Poem B presents [theme] through [method B]. The difference reveals [larger argument]." Drill the pattern.

Q27.2 paragraph architecture: Sophicly's house rule. One TTECEA paragraph per four marks. Q27.2 carries eight marks, so the answer is two TTECEA paragraphs at four marks each — not the integrated multi-hinge treatment the thirty-mark anthology Section B question rewards. Paragraph 1 is a full TTECEA on Poem A's method for the stem theme, anchored to a quoted line. Paragraph 2 is a full TTECEA on Poem B's method, opened by a contrast hinge of the form "Whereas Poem A uses [method A], Poem B uses [method B]", and closing on the comparison insight — what the difference in method reveals about the two poets' larger arguments. Each paragraph runs ~125 words. The whole response runs ~250 words. The sequential shape is faster to plan in the exam and easier for the examiner to follow.

## TTECEA+C paragraph shape — Sophicly's Grade-9 BP scaffold

Each Q27.1 body paragraph runs seven sentences. The structure is the same regardless of theme:

• Topic sentence (concept only) — concept anchored to a specific image or moment in the poem; zero techniques named. Max 30 words. The reader should be able to read sentence 1 and understand the paragraph's main claim about the poem.

• Technique — name the method (form / structure / language / sound), without quoting yet. One method per paragraph; resist multi-prong loading.

• Evidence — quote the anchor line. Ideally a single phrase, not a multi-line block. One anchor quote per paragraph.

• Close analysis — zoom into one specific word, sound, or image from the quote. Word-level not catalogue-level.

• Effects on reader — what the technique installs in the reader. Use precise emotion words; avoid generic "sadness" or "interest". Name the bodily, mental, or behavioural effect.

• Author's purpose — tentative, hedged ("may have written", "can be read as", "appears designed to"). Root the purpose in textual evidence, not biography.

• +C (alternative interpretation or counter-reading) — the generative tension. The same evidence supports a second reading that complicates the surface; this sentence stages that complication and is the paragraph's deepest move.

Two interchangeable BP architectures. Each Q deploys one of two three-paragraph spines, chosen to fit the poem's heaviest-loading element. Both architectures use the TTECEA+C internal shape; what differs is the axis along which the three BPs are organised.

• BEGINNING / MIDDLE / END (BME). BP1 anchors in the poem's opening, BP2 in the middle or volta, BP3 in the closing image. Use BME when the poem's argument unfolds in order — when the closing line genuinely changes what the opening meant. Q2, Q4, Q6, Q8 and Q10 in this bank use BME.

• FORM / STRUCTURE / LANGUAGE (FSL). BP1 zooms on form (the whole-poem shape — sonnet, couplet, stanza-shape, free verse), BP2 on structure (movements, voltic turn, closing pivot), BP3 on language (imagery, word choice, sound). Use FSL when the form itself does load-bearing work and the language cluster carries the deepest move. Q1, Q3, Q5, Q7 and Q9 in this bank use FSL.

Whichever architecture is chosen, BP3 carries the synthesising load — it is the paragraph that most often lands on the generative tension.

## How to substitute when AQA prints a different poem

The method is fixed; the poem is variable. The student walks into the exam holding:

• A method vocabulary (FLAME)

• A paragraph shape (TTECEA+C, seven sentences, three paragraphs)

• A method-comparison phrasing pattern (Sophicly's house formula)

• An emotion-word vocabulary (pity, dread, complicity, awe, tenderness, vertigo, indignation, exposure)

Whichever pair AQA prints in 2026, scan FLAME, identify three anchors, spine each BP through the heaviest-loading element using either the BEGINNING / MIDDLE / END or the FORM / STRUCTURE / LANGUAGE architecture, and write. Q27.2: write two sequential TTECEA paragraphs — paragraph 1 on Poem A's method, paragraph 2 on Poem B's method opened by a comparison hinge, each anchored to a quoted line.

## Memorise these once

Key terms (memorise once — useful across multiple pairs):

• FLAME — Sophicly's house acronym for the first-five-minutes method scan: Form, Language, Architecture, Music, Effect-on-reader.

• Controlling concept — the single claim the poem makes about its subject (not the subject itself). One sentence; if it cannot be stated in one sentence, it is not sharp enough.

• Volta — the structural turn; the moment the poem's argument pivots.

• Voltic structure — any poem that organises itself around a turn; the relationship between before-and-after is usually the controlling concept made structural.

• Cyclical structure — opening and closing on identical or near-identical material; usually argues the situation is unresolved or the lesson must be relearned.

• TTECEA+C — Sophicly's body-paragraph scaffold: Topic sentence, Technique, Evidence, Close analysis, Effect, Author's purpose, +Counter-reading.

• Method-first reading — the discipline of scanning for method during the second reading of the poem, before committing to content.

• Pararhyme — half-rhyme in which consonants match but vowels differ; produces refused resolution.

• Compound neologism — invented multi-word compound; enacts the failure of inherited vocabulary.

• Anaphora — insistent repetition at the start of successive lines or clauses; produces a chant-like rhythm.

• Apostrophe — direct address to an absent person or abstraction.

Universal AO2 phrasing pattern:

• A Band 4 essay says: "the poet uses alliteration."

• A Band 5 essay says: "the poet uses alliteration to replicate the percussion of gunfire."

• A Band 6 essay says: "the poet uses alliteration to replicate the percussion of gunfire, forcing the reader to hear the battlefield rather than read about it — collapsing aesthetic distance and conscripting the reader into the very experience the poem is documenting."

Sophicly content rules — academic register throughout; never use "shows" as analytical verb (use illustrates, portrays, demonstrates, emphasises, highlights, reveals, exposes, presents, conveys, enacts, signals, mirrors, dramatises, registers, captures, betrays); concrete prose connectors only (through, from, leading to, which produces); no arrows; topic sentences max 30 words concept-first; sentence cap ~40 words.

Q1 — 2017 Specimen — Regret across years — *I Am Very Bothered* (Armitage) + *Against Road Building* (Hannah)

In 'I Am Very Bothered', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about a past action? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — I Am Very Bothered (Simon Armitage)

I am very bothered when I think

of the bad things I have done in my life.

Not least that time in the chemistry lab

when I held a pair of scissors by the blades

and played the handles

in the naked lilac flame of the Bunsen burner;

then called your name, and handed them over.

O the unrivalled stench of branded skin

as you slipped your thumb and middle finger in,

then couldn't shake off the two burning rings. Marked,

the doctor said, for eternity.

Don't believe me, please, if I say

that was just my butterfingered way, at thirteen,

of asking you if you would marry me.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a confession of teenage cruelty. The generative tension is the closing tercet (three-line stanza): the speaker offers a marriage proposal as the misread motive for the burning. The poem stages regret AND, at the same time, refuses the usual shape of regret — it turns cruelty into a strained metaphor for desire. The Grade-9 reading does not read the closing as an excuse; it reads it as the speaker's continuing failure to accept what he did. The form (broken sonnet — fourteen lines split 7/4/3) carries the moral verdict: the love-sonnet shape is invoked and disfigured.

• Hook: "Simon Armitage was writing in 1989 from a Huddersfield-based probation-officer life inside a Britain whose post-Thatcher culture had begun to take confession seriously as a public form, from twelve-step recovery groups to victim-impact statements in the new Crown Court protocols. The book it appeared in addressed a generation reckoning with the long memory of casual schoolboy cruelty — the kind that the late-1980s mainstreaming of child-protection language had only just begun to name as harm rather than mischief."

• Building sentence: "The piece appeared in his debut collection Zoom! (1989), drafted during the years Armitage was working as a probation officer interviewing perpetrators of small but real harms. Behind the poem stood his own Marsden, West Yorkshire upbringing and the Northern grammar-school chemistry-lab world it draws on, in which Bunsen-burner cruelty had been an unremarked everyday risk."

• Thesis: "Across a fourteen-line sonnet broken into 7/4/3, a closing tercet whose proposal-frame fails the moment it is spoken, and burning-ring imagery that smuggles the wedding-band into the cruelty three stanzas early, Armitage stages the speaker's regret as a confession that still refuses the verdict it should deliver."

• Topic: The poem's chosen form is the love-sonnet disfigured, and the disfigurement itself carries the moral verdict before any line of content is read.

• Technique: Sonnet form invoked and broken — fourteen lines split 7/4/3 rather than the inherited octave-sestet, with the closing tercet refusing the formal couplet a love-sonnet expects.

• Evidence: "I am very bothered when I think / of the bad things I have done in my life. / Not least that time in the chemistry lab".

• Close analysis: The seven-line opening stanza overruns the standard eight-line opening of a sonnet by one and stops short by one — neither the inherited shape nor a clean refusal of it; the broken stanza-count signals a form trying to be faithful and failing.

• Effects: The reader registers the form's wrongness in the body — the count is wrong, the closing couplet that should land does not — and the wrongness prepares the reader for the moral wrongness the content will deliver.

• Author's purpose: Armitage may be using the love-sonnet form precisely because the closing turn will reveal the cruelty was later re-framed as courtship; the disfigured form is the formal evidence of the disfigured motive.

• +C: A counter-reading is that the broken sonnet refuses the comfort an unbroken sonnet would offer — the speaker is choosing the form whose breakage matches the harm done, signalling accountability through the refusal of the love-form he might once have claimed.

• Topic: The poem's structural turn occurs at the final tercet, where the previous eleven lines of confession are re-framed as a clumsy marriage proposal — and the re-frame is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Voltic structure (a sharp turn in the poem's argument) landing on a bracketed-feeling final disclosure, paired with the command "Don't believe me, please" that destabilises the closing claim before the speaker has made it.

• Evidence: "Don't believe me, please, if I say / that was just my butterfingered way, at thirteen, / of asking you if you would marry me."

• Close analysis: The volta lands at "if I say" — the conditional clause that introduces the proposal-frame as something the speaker knows will not be believed; "butterfingered way" makes the cruelty sound childlike, the compound adjective deflecting blame into clumsiness.

• Effects: The reader is placed as the juror of a defence the speaker himself is asking the juror to reject; the voltic structure produces revulsion rather than the tenderness the proposal-frame would otherwise invite, because the framing arrives already discredited.

• Author's purpose: Armitage appears to use the voltic closing to expose how perpetrators reshape past harm through later sentiment — the love-frame is staged as the speaker's defence mechanism, not the truth of the action.

• +C: A defensible counter-reading is that the speaker's pre-emptive disclaimer is sincere — he names the inadequacy of the proposal-frame because he knows it cannot redeem the act, and the volta's late arrival at "if I say" enacts a confession that never quite catches up with what was done.

• Topic: The poem's heaviest-loading language is the imagery of branding and rings in the central tercet, which plants the wedding-band symbolism three stanzas before the closing reveals the proposal-frame.

• Technique: Smell-and-touch sensory imagery loaded into the central stanza, with the noun "rings" doing double duty — burn-marks and wedding-bands at the same time.

• Evidence: "O the unrivalled stench of branded skin / as you slipped your thumb and middle finger in, / then couldn't shake off the two burning rings."

• Close analysis: The participle "branded" draws on the vocabulary of livestock-marking and slavery; the noun "rings" anticipates the wedding-bands the closing tercet will invoke; the verb "slipped" uses the wording of trying on jewellery, fusing the cruelty and the proposal at the level of word choice.

• Effects: The reader is forced into the chemistry lab through smell — smell-imagery is harder to refuse than visual imagery, because the reader cannot un-smell the imagined scene — and the bodily involvement prepares the recognition that the rings of injury are also the rings of marriage.

• Author's purpose: Armitage may be loading the language with proposal-vocabulary at the moment of injury to argue that the speaker's later re-framing was not a fabrication added afterwards but a continuous self-deception running through the action itself.

• +C: The generative tension lives at the level of language: "two burning rings" is the line where cruelty and courtship are already inseparable, and the poem's deepest move is the demonstration that the disfigured marriage-vocabulary was present in the original injury — the conversion was not added later but never absent.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Armitage deploys the disfigured sonnet, the sensory-loaded middle stanza, and the bracketed-feeling closing proposal to present regret as a confession that has not yet earned the verdict it deserves."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is its refusal of the redemption-arc that confessional poetry usually grants — the speaker remains, at the close, the author of the metaphor he cannot un-write.

In both 'I Am Very Bothered' and 'Against Road Building', the poets present feelings about an action that has consequences. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these feelings? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — Against Road Building (Sophie Hannah)

(Note: this comparison poem is named in the AQA Specimen Sample Set 1 teaching resources but is not in the public domain. Approach the methodological hinges below using the generic shape — if the actual paper prints a different second poem, the FLAME-scan + hinge-pair procedure carries across.)

• Topic sentence: Armitage presents the speaker's regret through second-person apostrophe (direct address to an absent person) to an absent victim, which stages the cruelty as a private intimacy the reader overhears rather than a public confession.

• Technique: Direct address to "you" sustained across the middle stanza, fusing victim-address with the reader's involuntary witness position.

• Evidence: "as you slipped your thumb and middle finger in, / then couldn't shake off the two burning rings."

• Close analysis: The pronoun "you" withholds the victim's name across the entire poem; the participle "slipped" converts the burning into something the victim is staged as having walked into, the address performing the speaker's continuing failure to take grammatical responsibility.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into a private intimacy the victim cannot now refuse — discomfort produced by the apostrophe placing the reader as the unauthorised audience.

• Author's purpose: Armitage may be using apostrophe to argue that later regret cannot escape the grammar of the original harm.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Armitage works through private apostrophe to a named victim, Hannah works through public address to a wider civic harm — the road-building project — registering regret as protest against an action not yet completed.

• Technique: Public direct address (reader-as-citizen) deployed in disciplined rhyming form, the form's order performing the moral position.

• Evidence: (per AQA Specimen Sample Set 1: Hannah's poem uses a structured rhyming voice to protest planned roadworks; the printed paper supplies the anchor line.)

• Close analysis: The disciplined rhyme converts the speaker's anger into careful argument rather than outburst; the public address places the reader as a fellow citizen with a stake in the outcome.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into argument rather than overhearing private cruelty — solidarity produced where Armitage produces discomfort.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Hannah may be using public address to make resistance possible while harm is still preventable; the difference reveals that Armitage's speaker addresses "you" across stanzas about "the two burning rings" already on a victim's hand, while Hannah's speaker addresses "you" across a rhymed civic argument about a road that has not yet been laid — the burning rings set against the road still on paper.

Q2 — June 2018 — Aging and its effects — *On Aging* (Maya Angelou) + *Jessie Emily Schofield* (Judy Williams)

In 'On Aging', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about getting older? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — On Aging (Maya Angelou)

When you see me sitting quietly,

Like a sack left on the shelf,

Don't think I need your chattering.

I'm listening to myself.

Hold! Stop! Don't pity me!

Hold! Stop your sympathy!

Understanding if you got it,

Otherwise I'll do without it!

When my bones are stiff and aching,

And my feet won't climb the stair,

I will only ask one favor:

Don't bring me no rocking chair.

When you see me walking, stumbling,

Don't study and get it wrong.

'Cause tired don't mean lazy

And every goodbye ain't gone.

I'm the same person I was back then,

A little less hair, a little less chin,

A lot less lungs and much less wind.

But ain't I lucky I can still breathe in.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a defiant rejection of pity. The generative tension is the closing quatrain's tonal turn from commands ("Hold! Stop!") to gratitude ("ain't I lucky I can still breathe in") — the speaker who refused sympathy has nothing left to refuse except thankfulness for the breath itself. The poem's controlling concept is not anti-pity but pro-dignity; Angelou converts the older speaker from someone cared for into the author of her own life, and the form (cascading commands giving way to plain lyric statement) enacts the turn.

• Hook: "Maya Angelou was writing in 1978 from a public American literary life that had begun with I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings (1969), inside a post-civil-rights United States in which Black women's voices had only recently been admitted to the mainstream lyric tradition and in which older Black women remained particularly absent from the published record of self-portraiture. The book it appeared in addressed a culture whose dominant images of the older Black woman had been drawn either as silent maternal endurance or as folk-comic stereotype, leaving the dignified first-person commanding voice quietly unrepresented."

• Building sentence: "The piece appeared in her third poetry collection And Still I Rise (1978), composed in the years immediately after her appointment to the first chair of American Studies at Wake Forest University and her continuing post-Stamps-Arkansas reckoning with the rural Black-American grandmother figures who had raised her. Behind the poem stood her own grandmother Annie Henderson, whose general-store life in Depression-era Arkansas had shaped Angelou's sense of how an older Black woman might assert authority through plain speech rather than through inherited prestige forms."

• Thesis: "Across cascading commands that refuse the witness's right to interpret stillness, plain-speech word choice that refuses the standard literary style, and a closing quatrain that converts the catalogue of loss into thankfulness for the breath itself, Angelou stages the speaker's feelings about aging as a defiant reclaiming of self that earns the right to its final humility."

• Topic: The opening stanza claims the speaker's right to her inner life by refusing the witness's right to interpret her stillness.

• Technique: Cascading commands loaded back-to-back with exclamation marks, paired with the simile that names the misreading.

• Evidence: "When you see me sitting quietly, / Like a sack left on the shelf, / Don't think I need your chattering. / I'm listening to myself. / Hold! Stop! Don't pity me!"

• Close analysis: The simile "a sack left on the shelf" names the misreading the witness makes — stillness mistaken for emptiness; the commands "Hold! Stop!" stage the speaker's refusal of the misreading the moment it begins.

• Effects: The reader is placed as the chattering witness — the "you" implicates whoever is reading — and the commands produce shock rather than recognition; the reader must reposition before the second stanza begins.

• Author's purpose: Angelou may be staging the commands as a rhetorical recovery of authority — the speaker who would be overlooked claims the grammar of command, refusing the passive role the witness assigned.

• +C: The cascading commands can also be read as the speaker's exhaustion at having to defend the obvious — the volume signalling the cumulative weight of being misread across years.

• Topic: The middle stanzas catalogue physical limit without surrender, claiming dignity through the refusal of one specific symbol of passive old age.

• Technique: "When"-clauses ("When my bones are stiff and aching") plus plain-speech word choice ("don't bring me no rocking chair") refusing standard English.

• Evidence: "When my bones are stiff and aching, / And my feet won't climb the stair, / I will only ask one favor: / Don't bring me no rocking chair."

• Close analysis: The rocking chair is a cultural symbol of an old age that has retired into passivity; the double negative "don't bring me no" refuses both the chair and the style of language the chair belongs to — a style that would silence the speaker into a different grammar than her own.

• Effects: The reader registers the plain-speech as the speaker's chosen authority — her dignity lives inside her language, not above it; the double negative produces respect rather than correction.

• Author's purpose: Angelou appears to fuse African-American plain-speech with the lyric form to assert that the speaker's authority does not need to be translated into standard English; the form's hybrid nature is the argument.

• +C: The rocking chair as the only refused object suggests that the speaker accepts every other physical limit aging brings — her dignity is selective, not absolute; she names exactly what she will not become.

• Topic: The closing quatrain converts the defiance of the opening into gratitude for the most basic continuance, and the conversion is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Tonal turn from command to flat statement paired with the rhetorical question that closes the poem.

• Evidence: "I'm the same person I was back then, / A little less hair, a little less chin, / A lot less lungs and much less wind. / But ain't I lucky I can still breathe in."

• Close analysis: The anaphora (repeated opening phrase) "A little less... A little less... A lot less... much less" enacts the cumulative loss of aging, then the conjunction "But" turns the catalogue into the rhetorical question "ain't I lucky" — the speaker rewriting subtraction as luck.

• Effects: The reader experiences relief at the tonal turn — the commands have done their work and the speaker is no longer defending against the witness but speaking with her; the rhetorical question recruits the reader into the gratitude rather than performing it for them.

• Author's purpose: Angelou may be arguing that dignity in aging is not the refusal of limit but the right to name limit on the speaker's own terms; the closing's gratitude is earned by the opening's defiance.

• +C: The generative tension lies in the conversion: the speaker who refused pity at the opening claims luck at the close, and the two positions are not contradictions but stages of the same dignity. The poem argues that gratitude is what authority finally permits the older speaker to feel.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Angelou deploys cascading commands, plain-speech 'when'-clauses, and a tonal turn from defiance to gratitude to present aging as a process the speaker authors rather than a process performed on her."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the conversion of refusal into thankfulness — dignity in aging is not the absence of decline but the speaker's right to name the decline in her own grammar.

In both 'On Aging' and 'Jessie Emily Schofield', the speakers consider getting older. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present aging? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — Jessie Emily Schofield (Judy Williams)

I used to wash my grandmother's hair,

When she was old and small

And walked with a frame

Like a learning child.

She would turn off her hearing aid

And bend into the water,

Holding the edge of the sink with long fingers;

I would pour warm cupfuls over her skull

And wonder what it could be like

In her deaf head with eighty years of life.

Hers was the softest hair I ever felt,

Wedding dress silk on a widow;

But there is a photo of her

Sitting swathed in hair

That I imagine chestnut from the black and white,

Long enough to sit on.

Her wet head felt delicate as a birdskull

Worn thin by waves of age,

As she stood bent.

My mother's mother under my hands.

• Topic sentence: Angelou presents aging through first-person command voice spoken by the older subject herself, the grammar of command claiming authority that the witness has attempted to withhold.

• Technique: Command voice cascaded across stacked exclamations, staging the older speaker as agent rather than object.

• Evidence: "Hold! Stop! Don't pity me! / Hold! Stop your sympathy!"

• Close analysis: The single-syllable "Hold! Stop!" refuses ornament; the command "Don't pity me!" claims the grammar of command, refusing the passive position the witness had assigned.

• Effects: The reader is repositioned as the chattering witness whose interpretation must now be retracted; respect produced through the reversal in grammar.

• Author's purpose: Angelou may be using command voice to argue that dignity in aging requires the older speaker to claim the grammar that pity would otherwise occupy on her behalf.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Angelou works through the older subject's own command voice, Williams works through the witness's first-person observational voice tending the older subject — aging registered as something the speaker speaks TOWARDS rather than FROM.

• Technique: First-person past-tense observation loaded with touch-imagery, the speaker placed as caregiver narrating the older body's frailty.

• Evidence: "Her wet head felt delicate as a birdskull / Worn thin by waves of age."

• Close analysis: The simile "delicate as a birdskull" converts the older body into something held rather than commanded; "worn thin by waves of age" registers the wearing-down of time through a natural-force metaphor, softening the frailty without sentimentalising it.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into care rather than instructed in respect — tenderness produced through touch-imagery.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Williams may be using witness-voice touch-imagery to argue that aging is borne in part by those who tend it; the difference reveals that Angelou's speaker barks single-syllable commands at the witness ("Hold! Stop!"), while Williams's witness reaches a hand to a head that feels like a "birdskull / Worn thin by waves of age" — the older speaker's voice answering back set against the carer's hand on the older body.

Q3 — June 2019 — How to live your life — *The Richest Poor Man in the Valley* (Lindsay Macrae) + *Nobody* (Michael Laskey)

In 'The Richest Poor Man in the Valley', how does the poet present the speaker's attitude to how a life should be lived? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — The Richest Poor Man in the Valley (Lindsay Macrae)

On the outside

he seemed older than he was.

His face was like a weather map

full of bad weather

while inside

his heart was fat with sun.

With his two dogs

he cleared a thin silver path

across the Black Mountain.

And when winter

kicked in

they brought his sheep

down from the top

like sulky clouds.

Harry didn't care for things

that other people prize

like money, houses, bank accounts

and lies.

He was living in a caravan

until the day he died.

But at his funeral

his friends' tears

fell like a thousand

diamonds.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a celebration of a life lived simply, away from material wealth. The generative tension is the closing image: the funeral tears become "a thousand diamonds" — the conventional materialism the poem rejected is brought back as the measure of Harry's wealth, but turned from possession into mourning. The poem argues that the only true wealth is what others lose when you die. The form (irregular free-verse stanzas of accumulating length) enacts the slow accumulation of a life's worth made visible only at its end.

• Hook: "Lindsay Macrae was writing in the late 1990s and early 2000s from a UK children's-poetry and adult-poetry crossover position, inside a late-Thatcher-into-Blair Britain whose consumer-credit culture had quietly turned material accumulation into the default measure of an adult life. The book it appeared in addressed a generation in which the rural valley elegy had become an unfashionable form precisely because the late-twentieth-century literary marketplace rewarded urban irony over rural attention."

• Building sentence: "The piece appeared in her adult-poetry work after a decade of acclaimed children's collections including How to Avoid Kissing Your Parents in Public (2000), in which Macrae had built her ear for the cadence of a non-affected speaking voice. Behind the poem stood the small-valley funeral culture of post-mining and post-farming British rural communities, in which the absence of material wealth had become the conventional embarrassment to mourn before the actual person could be named."

• Thesis: "Across irregular accumulating free-verse stanzas, a voltic move from biography into the closing simile, and weather-and-jewellery imagery that converts the rejected world's vocabulary into the only adequate accounting of love, Macrae stages the speaker's attitude to how a life should be lived as a defence of meaning measured by absence rather than by accumulation."

• Topic: The poem's chosen form — irregular free-verse stanzas of accumulating length — enacts the slow accumulation of a life whose worth is visible only when the whole is in view.

• Technique: Free verse organised into five stanzas of unpredictable line-count (6, 7, 4, 2, 4), the absence of inherited regularity placing the formal weight on the shape of the stanzas rather than the metre.

• Evidence: "He was living in a caravan / until the day he died."

• Close analysis: The stand-alone couplet sits between the dismissive list ("money, houses, bank accounts / and lies") and the closing diamonds — two lines bracketed by white space, the form making Harry's poverty impossible to ignore; the couplet's brevity refuses to soften the fact with explanation.

• Effects: The reader notices that the form refuses to summarise — Harry's life arrives in stanza-fragments rather than as a connected argument, and the stand-alone couplet produces a moment of arrested attention the surrounding stanzas cannot bury.

• Author's purpose: Macrae may be using irregular free verse to argue that lives lived outside conventional measures resist conventional formal shapes — the form's refusal of regularity matches Harry's refusal of accumulation.

• +C: A counter-reading is that the variable stanza-lengths register the speaker's emotional rhythm rather than Harry's life — the form is mourner-shaped, not subject-shaped, and the stand-alone couplet is the speaker's reluctance to move past Harry's poverty rather than Harry's reluctance to leave the caravan.

• Topic: The poem's structural turn occurs at the closing simile, where the wealth-vocabulary the previous four stanzas had rejected returns turned from possession into mourning — and the structural inversion is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Voltic structure landing on a closing simile, with the conjunction "But" opening the final stanza and signalling the re-frame the poem has been building toward.

• Evidence: "But at his funeral / his friends' tears / fell like a thousand / diamonds."

• Close analysis: The conjunction "But" refuses the closure the previous stanzas seemed to offer (Harry's death in a caravan); the line-break between "thousand" and "diamonds" (enjambment, where a sentence runs over the line ending) holds back the measure until the final word, making the reader feel the weight of the figure before its name arrives.

• Effects: The reader experiences structural relief — the rejected vocabulary returns as the only adequate accounting; the inversion produces a particular elation rather than sentimental grief, because the structural work has earned the simile.

• Author's purpose: Macrae may be using the voltic closing to argue that the language of value the reader brings to the poem cannot be discarded — only redirected to count the right things; structural inversion is how the redirection is performed.

• +C: The generative tension is the relationship between rejection and re-use: the poem refuses materialism through four stanzas yet structures its argument's climax around the most material of all images — the rhetorical move admits that meaning cannot be made without the vocabulary the speaker disowns. Materialism is the poem's tool and its target at the same time.

• Topic: The poem's heaviest-loading language is the pairing of pathetic-fallacy weather imagery in the opening with a connective-free list of rejected materialism in the middle — two contrasting clusters of imagery that prepare the diamonds-simile to land.

• Technique: Pathetic-fallacy simile (face-as-weather-map) in the opening, a connective-free list (asyndeton) of rejected nouns in the middle, the two clusters working as opposed word-families.

• Evidence: "His face was like a weather map / full of bad weather / while inside / his heart was fat with sun." paired with "like money, houses, bank accounts / and lies."

• Close analysis: "Fat with sun" yokes an adjective of material plenty to the most immaterial source of wealth available, fusing the poem's two registers (its two ways of speaking) in a single image; the connective-free list ends with "lies" — three concrete material nouns and one abstraction slipped in at the close, the syntax converting the list into critique inside a single sentence.

• Effects: The reader experiences a moral re-tuning produced at the level of word-choice — the opening's interior brightness and the middle's list of falsehoods prepare the reader to accept the closing's turned-around diamonds.

• Author's purpose: Macrae may be loading the imagery to argue that the world's vocabulary can be ethically re-used — weather-vocabulary registers shelter, jewellery-vocabulary registers mourning, list-vocabulary registers critique.

• +C: A defensible counter-reading is that "bad weather" names real hardship the poem refuses to soften — Harry's brightness lives alongside the weather, not in defeat of it. The poem holds two positions in its language: Harry's wealth was his life's argument; Harry's poverty was its cost. The imagery does not abolish hardship, it accompanies it.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Macrae deploys pathetic-fallacy weather imagery, a connective-free list of materialism rejected, and a final simile that converts diamonds from possession into mourning, presenting how a life should be lived as a defence of meaning measured by absence rather than by accumulation."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the recognition that the world's measures of wealth cannot be discarded — only redirected to count the right things.

In both 'The Richest Poor Man in the Valley' and 'Nobody', the speakers present ideas about how to live a life. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these ideas? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — Nobody (Michael Laskey)

If you can't bring yourself to build

a snowman or even to clench

a snowball or two to fling

at the pine tree trunk, at least

find some reason to take you

out of yourself: scrape a patch

of grass clear for the birds maybe;

prod at your shrubs so they shake off

the weight, straighten up; or just

stump about leaving prints

of your boots, your breath steaming out.

Promise.

Don't let yourself in for this moment

again: the end of the afternoon,

drawing the curtains on the glare

of the garden, a whole day of snow

nobody's trodden.

• Topic sentence: Macrae presents the argument through third-person past-tense biography, the reader placed outside Harry's completed life and invited to weigh it once the accounting is final.

• Technique: Third-person past-tense narration loaded with the list of rejected materialism, the grammar holding the reader at observational distance.

• Evidence: "Harry didn't care for things / that other people prize / like money, houses, bank accounts / and lies."

• Close analysis: The past-tense "didn't care" converts Harry into completed argument; the connective-free list ending on "lies" converts the list into critique without grammatical signposting.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into admiration — biography produces respect at distance.

• Author's purpose: Macrae may be using completed-biography grammar to argue that the question of how to live is best answered by what an absence cost.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Macrae writes in completed third-person past-tense biography, Laskey writes in second-person command present, addressing the reader directly with instructions to be acted on today.

• Technique: Second-person command present, accumulating across the opening stanza, fused with the warning that closes the poem.

• Evidence: "at least / find some reason to take you / out of yourself: scrape a patch / of grass clear for the birds maybe."

• Close analysis: The command "find some reason" converts the reader into the agent the poem is addressing; "at least" registers the speaker's awareness that the bar is low and the failure is common.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into action — instruction produces tonight's behavioural change rather than admiration at distance.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Laskey may be using command-instruction grammar to argue that the question of how to live is best answered by tonight's regret avoided; the difference reveals that Macrae writes in completed past tense looking back at Harry's funeral, while Laskey writes in present-tense commands telling the reader to "scrape a patch / of grass clear for the birds maybe" before bed — Macrae's funeral set against Laskey's small task to perform tonight.

Q4 — November 2020 — Feelings about love — *I Am Offering this Poem* (Jimmy Santiago Baca) + *The sun has burst the sky* (Jenny Joseph)

In 'I Am Offering this Poem', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about love? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — I Am Offering this Poem (Jimmy Santiago Baca)

I am offering this poem to you,

since I have nothing else to give.

Keep it like a warm coat

when winter comes to cover you,

or like a pair of thick socks

the cold cannot bite through,

I love you,

I have nothing else to give you,

so it is a pot full of yellow corn

to warm your belly in winter,

it is a scarf for your head, to wear

over your hair, to tie up around your face,

I love you,

Keep it, treasure this as you would

if you were lost, needing direction,

in the wilderness life becomes when mature;

and in the corner of your drawer,

tucked away like a cabin or hogan

in dense trees, come knocking,

and I will answer, give you directions,

and let you warm yourself by this fire,

rest by this fire, and make you feel safe

I love you,

It's all I have to give,

and all anyone needs to live,

and to go on living inside,

when the world outside

no longer cares if you live or die;

remember,

I love you.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a love poem offered as a gift in the absence of possessions. The generative tension is the closing's pivot from object-imagery to moral instruction — "all anyone needs to live" — the poem converting from private offering into public claim. The form (accumulating extended-metaphor stanzas with the refrain "I love you" set off between them) enacts the offering's accumulation; the speaker has nothing material to give, but the poem itself becomes the accumulated material objects through metaphor.

• Hook: "Jimmy Santiago Baca was writing in the late 1970s from a New Mexico prison cell at the Arizona State Prison in Florence, having taught himself to read and write inside, in a United States in which Chicano-American voices had only recently been admitted to the published literary mainstream after the Rodolfo Gonzales / I Am Joaquín (1967) generation. The book it appeared in addressed a culture in which incarcerated voices — particularly Mexican-American ones — had been treated as unworthy of the love-lyric tradition, and in which the gift-poem from someone with nothing material to offer carried a documentary as well as a poetic weight."

• Building sentence: "The piece appeared in his debut collection Immigrants in Our Own Land (1979), composed inside the six and a half years he served on drug-possession charges before being released to a literary life that would eventually include the American Book Award. Behind the poem stood his own Chicano-American childhood — orphaned at five, raised across orphanages, foster homes, and street life in Albuquerque — and the prison literacy programme that had introduced him to the inherited love-lyric tradition he was now rewriting from the inside."

• Thesis: "Across an accumulating sequence of practical-object metaphors that name the body's basic needs in cold, an extended cabin-in-trees metaphor that converts the offering from clothing into shelter, and a closing pivot from personal pronoun to universal 'anyone', Baca stages the speaker's feelings about love as a substitute for material wealth that becomes the only adequate currency for survival."

• Topic: The opening stanza establishes the poem's central conceit (extended metaphor) — that the poem itself can substitute for the practical materials the speaker cannot otherwise provide.

• Technique: Sequence of practical-object similes loaded against the apologetic frame.

• Evidence: "Keep it like a warm coat / when winter comes to cover you, / or like a pair of thick socks / the cold cannot bite through."

• Close analysis: Both objects are functional rather than decorative — "warm coat", "thick socks" — naming the body's most basic needs in cold; the modifier "the cold cannot bite through" personifies cold as predator, raising the stakes of the offering.

• Effects: The reader hears the "thick socks" and "warm coat" as survival kit rather than gift — this is love expressed as survival aid, not as decoration; the effect is tenderness mixed with anxiety, because the speaker has named the cold the offering must hold off.

• Author's purpose: Baca may be using the practical-object similes to mark the speaker's material poverty without naming it directly — the poem becomes the things the speaker cannot afford to buy.

• +C: The counter-reading is that the practical-object frame is itself the love — the speaker is offering not a substitute for material but the recognition that material things WERE always love, made visible by their absence.

• Topic: The middle stanzas convert the offered object from clothing into shelter, and the conversion stages love as direction-giving when the world fails.

• Technique: Extended cabin-in-trees metaphor paired with the command "come knocking" that turns the metaphor into a code of mutual obligation.

• Evidence: "in the corner of your drawer, / tucked away like a cabin or hogan / in dense trees, come knocking, / and I will answer."

• Close analysis: "Cabin or hogan" offers two cultural framings at once — Anglo cabin and Navajo hogan — both naming shelter in wilderness; the command "come knocking" converts the poem from passive keepsake into active relationship, the speaker offering not a thing but availability.

• Effects: The reader experiences the shift from clothing-imagery to shelter-imagery as a deepening of the offering — the poem now provides not just warmth but a place; the two cultural framings (cabin or hogan) refuse to specify, allowing the offering to reach across community borders.

• Author's purpose: Baca appears to use the dual-cultural shelter image to argue that love refuses ethnic separation — the poem is offering itself as shelter for whoever needs it, regardless of inheritance.

• +C: The image "tucked away like a cabin or hogan / in dense trees" admits the reading that the poem must be hidden to function — love survives only when it is kept out of sight of the "world outside" the closing names; the dense trees are protection, not just setting.

• Topic: The closing stanza turns from personal offering into a moral statement that applies to anyone, and the turn is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Conversion of the personal pronoun "you" into the universal "anyone", paired with the negative "when"-clause "when the world outside / no longer cares if you live or die".

• Evidence: "It's all I have to give, / and all anyone needs to live, / and to go on living inside, / when the world outside / no longer cares if you live or die."

• Close analysis: The shift from "I have to give" to "anyone needs to live" converts the speaker from a particular lover to a universal advocate; the conditional "when the world outside / no longer cares" names the precise public condition under which the poem must function — being abandoned by the wider social order.

• Effects: The reader hears the stake widen — what began as a love-gift between two has become an argument about what sustains any person against indifference; the rhetorical move converts the reader from witness to recipient.

• Author's purpose: Baca may be arguing that love is not a private accessory but a public minimum — the only thing that allows continued living when public institutions fail.

• +C: The generative tension is that the poem began as a confession of having "nothing else to give" — material poverty — yet ends by claiming the offering is "all anyone needs". The poverty has been turned into sufficiency; the lack the poem began with is, by the close, the precondition of the abundance it offers.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Baca deploys accumulating practical-object metaphors, a refrain that punctuates each stanza, and a final universal turn to present the speaker's feelings about love as the only adequate currency for survival when public institutions fail."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the conversion of material poverty into moral wealth — what the speaker cannot afford to give materially becomes precisely what proves love's sufficiency.

In both 'I Am Offering this Poem' and 'The sun has burst the sky', the speakers present their feelings about love. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these feelings? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — The sun has burst the sky (Jenny Joseph)

The sun has burst the sky

Because I love you

And the river its banks.

The sea laps the great rocks

Because I love you

And takes no heed of the moon dragging it away

And saying coldly 'Constancy is not for you'.

The blackbird fills the air

Because I love you

With spring and lawns and shadows falling on lawns.

The people walk in the street and laugh

I love you

And far down the river ships sound their hooters

Crazy with joy because I love you.

• Topic sentence: Baca presents love as the offering of survival itself, the speaker's poverty converted into the list of necessities the gift must replace.

• Technique: Accumulating practical-object similes (clothing, food, shelter), each grounded in the body's basic needs in cold weather.

• Evidence: "Keep it like a warm coat / when winter comes to cover you, / or like a pair of thick socks / the cold cannot bite through."

• Close analysis: "Warm coat" and "thick socks" are functional rather than decorative — naming survival rather than romance; the personification "the cold cannot bite through" casts cold as predator and raises the offering's stakes from sentiment to defence.

• Effects: The reader hears the "thick socks" and "warm coat" as survival kit rather than gift — tenderness mixed with anxiety, because the speaker has named the cold the offering must hold off.

• Author's purpose: Baca may be using practical-object imagery to argue that love supplies what is materially absent; the poem becomes the things the speaker cannot afford to buy.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Baca presents love through practical-material substitutes for what is missing, Joseph presents love through natural cosmic events, converting the speaker's emotion into the breaking of natural limits.

• Technique: Repeated "Because" clauses (anaphora — repeated opening phrase) — "Because I love you" repeated as the cause of cosmic effects — fused with natural-event imagery.

• Evidence: "The sun has burst the sky / Because I love you / And the river its banks."

• Close analysis: The verb "burst" converts love into rupture rather than offering; "Because I love you" repeated places love as cosmic cause, the speaker the source rather than the giver.

• Effects: The reader hears love as the cause of the sky bursting and the river breaking its banks — exhilaration rather than the anxious tenderness Baca's necessity-imagery produces.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Joseph may be using cosmic-cause imagery to argue that love is the condition the speaker exists in, not the action the speaker performs; the difference reveals that Baca's love arrives as a "warm coat" and "thick socks the cold cannot bite through" — items handed across, item by item, set against Joseph's love that has "burst the sky" and broken the river over its banks before the poem begins.

Q5 — November 2021 — A city at night — *A London Thoroughfare. 2 A.M.* (Amy Lowell) + *November night, Edinburgh* (Norman MacCaig)

In 'A London Thoroughfare. 2 A.M.', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about the city at night? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — A London Thoroughfare. 2 A.M. (Amy Lowell)

They have watered the street,

It shines in the glare of lamps,

Cold, white lamps,

And lies

Like a slow-moving river,

Barred with silver and black.

Cabs go down it,

One,

And then another,

Between them I hear the shuffling of feet.

Tramps doze on the window-ledges,

Night-walkers pass along the sidewalks.

The city is squalid and sinister,

With the silver-barred street in the midst,

Slow-moving,

A river leading nowhere.

Opposite my window,

The moon cuts,

Clear and round,

Through the plum-coloured night.

She cannot light the city:

It is too bright.

It has white lamps,

And glitters coldly.

I stand in the window and watch the moon.

She is thin and lustreless,

But I love her.

I know the moon,

And this is an alien city.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a description of a city at night seen from a window. The generative tension is the closing volta — the speaker's recognition of the moon ("I know the moon") and rejection of the city ("this is an alien city") — the poem converting from observation into estrangement. The form (free verse with deliberate single-word and short-line breaks that mimic the cabs going past one at a time) enacts the speaker's piece-by-piece taking-in of the city she cannot belong to.

• Hook: "Amy Lowell was writing in 1914 from a Boston-Brahmin literary life that had just taken her to London for an Imagist summer alongside Ezra Pound, inside a moment in which Anglo-American modernism was abandoning Victorian ornamental verse in favour of clean visual lines and free-verse cadence. The book it appeared in addressed a transatlantic readership in which the visiting American poet's view of the London night had become, briefly, a recognised sub-genre — and in which electric street lighting was new enough that its visual aggression could still be registered as estrangement rather than ambient given."

• Building sentence: "The piece appeared in her breakthrough collection Sword Blades and Poppy Seed (1914), composed during the visits to London in 1913 and 1914 that placed her at the centre of the Imagist movement before her public quarrel with Pound led to her editing the subsequent Some Imagist Poets anthologies. Behind the poem stood her own privileged Brookline, Massachusetts upbringing — sister of Harvard astronomer Percival Lowell — and a literary education in which the inherited Victorian streetscape elegies of Tennyson and Arnold had quietly become the wrong vocabulary for the electrified modern city."

• Thesis: "Across free verse whose deliberate single-word and short-line breaks mimic the cabs going past one at a time, a voltic closing turn converting observation into declared estrangement, and a cluster of cold-colour words that names artificial light as visually aggressive rather than illuminating, Lowell stages the speaker's feelings about the city at night as an estrangement that the artificial light cannot reach."

• Topic: The poem's chosen form is free verse organised through deliberately uneven short lines and isolated single-word lines, the form's broken-up shape enacting the speaker's piece-by-piece taking-in of the city she cannot belong to.

• Technique: Free verse with unpredictable line-length variation — line-lengths swing from four syllables to ten — and stand-alone single-syllable lines ("One,", "And lies") that withhold completion.

• Evidence: "Cabs go down it, / One, / And then another, / Between them I hear the shuffling of feet."

• Close analysis: "One," — a single word followed by a comma standing as its own line — visually enacts the cab passing alone, the brevity refusing the comfort of grammatical continuation; the line-break before "And then another" makes the reader wait for the next cab the way the speaker does.

• Effects: The reader registers the form in the body as the rhythm of windowed watching — each line a discrete unit, the wait between them part of the experience the form transmits.

• Author's purpose: Lowell may be using broken-up free verse to argue that the modern city is experienced as fragments rather than narrative — the unconnected lines mirror the unconnected sights they catalogue.

• +C: A counter-reading is that the free-verse fragments register the speaker's careful attention rather than the city's failure — the lines slow her reading down, allowing close attention; the form's discontinuity is what licences her seeing.

• Topic: The poem's structural turn occurs at the closing volta, where observation converts into declaration — the speaker chooses the moon she knows over the city she does not — and the structural choice is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Voltic structure landing on a paired flat statement — "I know the moon, / And this is an alien city" — preceded by the conjunction "But" that signals the turn.

• Evidence: "She is thin and lustreless, / But I love her. / I know the moon, / And this is an alien city."

• Close analysis: The structural turn occurs at "But" — the conjunction the previous twenty-six lines have not used; the closing paired statement refuses qualification, the volta arriving as verdict rather than discovery.

• Effects: The reader experiences the structural arrival as moral clarity — the speaker has named her preference and what she rejects; the effect is identification rather than spectacle.

• Author's purpose: Lowell may be using the voltic structure to argue that knowledge — what is recognised across time — is the precondition of belonging; the alien city is alien precisely because nothing in it is known the way the moon is known.

• +C: The generative tension lies in love and rejection happening at once — the speaker loves a "thin and lustreless" moon and rejects a city that "glitters coldly". Brightness is no recommendation; lustre is no recommendation. The voltic structure argues that the speaker's allegiance follows her knowledge, not her seeing.

• Topic: The poem's heaviest-loading language is a sustained cluster of cold-colour words that converts the wet street into a metallic prison and the moon-versus-lamps rivalry into an explicit verdict on artificial light.

• Technique: Repeated colour and temperature words building a single word-family — "Cold", "white", "silver", "black", "plum-coloured", "coldly" — fused with personifications of moon and city.

• Evidence: "Cold, white lamps, / And lies / Like a slow-moving river, / Barred with silver and black." paired with "She cannot light the city: / It is too bright."

• Close analysis: "Barred with silver and black" converts the street's reflections into prison-imagery — the bars of light and shadow alternating; "too bright" exposes the city's sickness — light so excessive that the moon becomes invisible; the colon stages the verdict.

• Effects: The reader experiences vertigo rather than nostalgia — the natural landmark that organises night has been overruled by the artificial, and the cold-colour vocabulary cements the city as something done TO the speaker rather than seen BY her.

• Author's purpose: Lowell may be loading the cold-colour cluster to register the modern city's specific harm — not noise, not crime, but the obliteration of natural landmarks by which a body knows where it is.

• +C: The image "plum-coloured night" admits the reading that the speaker has not yet lost her capacity for natural seeing — the colour-word is a Romantic style of language the city has not erased, and the moon's "cutting" remains visible to her even when it cannot operate as light. The language argues that her capacity to see exceeds her city's capacity to drown it.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Lowell deploys a cold-colour cluster, free-verse line-breaks mimicking the piece-by-piece taking-in of the cityscape, and a voltic recognition of the moon against the alien city to present the speaker's feelings about the city at night as estrangement that artificial light cannot reach."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the demonstration that belonging follows knowledge, not brightness — what the speaker knows orients her, regardless of how dim it has become.

In both 'A London Thoroughfare. 2 A.M.' and 'November night, Edinburgh', the speakers describe a city at night. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these settings? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — November night, Edinburgh (Norman MacCaig)

The night tinkles like ice in glasses.

Leaves are glued to the pavement with frost.

The brown air fumes at the shop windows,

Tries the doors, and sidles past.

I gulp down winter raw.

The heady

Darkness swirls with tenements.

In a brown fuzz of cottonwool

Lamps fade up crags, die into pits.

Frost in my lungs is harsh as leaves

Scraped up on paths.

— I look up, there,

A high roof sails, at the mast-head

Fluttering a grey and ragged star.

The world's a bear shrugged in his den.

It's snug and close in the snoring night.

And outside like chrysanthemums

The fog unfolds its bitter scent.

• Topic sentence: Lowell presents the city through cold-colour visual imagery sustained as a word-cluster, the reader positioned behind glass to watch but not inhabit.

• Technique: Cold-colour word-cluster (silver, black, white, cold) loaded across the poem's free-verse shape, fused with sustained visual distance.

• Evidence: "Cold, white lamps, / And lies / Like a slow-moving river, / Barred with silver and black."

• Close analysis: The triad "Cold, white lamps" refuses tonal warmth at the word-level; "Barred with silver and black" converts the street into a prison of alternating reflections, the bars naming containment without naming a body inside them.

• Effects: The reader is positioned as witness behind glass — vertigo without touch.

• Author's purpose: Lowell may be using sustained visual distance to argue that the modern city's harm is the wiping-out of the natural landmarks by which a body knows where it is.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Lowell presents the city through cold visual imagery observed at distance, MacCaig presents the city through synaesthetic body-imagery (synaesthesia — mixing of senses) — the reader inhales the city through the speaker's body.

• Technique: Synaesthetic body-imagery (taste, breath, touch) fused with animal metaphor in the closing.

• Evidence: "I gulp down winter raw." paired with "The world's a bear shrugged in his den. / It's snug and close in the snoring night."

• Close analysis: "Gulp down winter raw" converts cold into eaten substance — winter swallowed rather than seen; "a bear shrugged in his den" converts urban cold into sleeping-animal warmth, the metaphor re-framing the same temperature as shelter.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into snugness — accommodation produced through body-imagery where Lowell produces estrangement.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: MacCaig may be using body-and-animal metaphor imagery to argue that urban night can be made bearable through the speaker's metaphorical re-framing; the difference reveals that Lowell's speaker stands behind glass watching "cold, white lamps" and "silver and black" bars of street-reflection, while MacCaig's speaker is inside the city "gulp[ing] down winter raw" and folded into a "bear shrugged in his den" — Lowell's metallic glass set against MacCaig's swallowed cold and sleeping animal.

Q6 — June 2022 — Care and responsibility — *Shoulders* (Naomi Shihab Nye) + *Choices* (Tess Gallagher)

In 'Shoulders', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about caring for someone vulnerable? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — Shoulders (Naomi Shihab Nye)

A man crosses the street in rain,

stepping gently, looking two times north and south,

because his son is asleep on his shoulder.

No car must splash him.

No car drive too near to his shadow.

This man carries the world's most sensitive cargo

but he's not marked.

Nowhere does his jacket say FRAGILE,

HANDLE WITH CARE.

His ear fills up with breathing.

He hears the hum of a boy's dream

deep inside him.

We're not going to be able to live in this world

if we're not willing to do what he's doing

with one another.

The road will only be wide.

The rain will never stop falling.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is an observation of a father carrying his sleeping son across a rainy street. The generative tension is the turn from one particular father to a moral claim about everyone ("We're not going to be able to live in this world") — the poem converting from observation into a public argument. The form (free verse with the central command and the universal claim deliberately set in their own stanzas) enacts the conversion from witness to instruction.

• Hook: "Naomi Shihab Nye was writing in 1994 from a San Antonio, Texas household combining Palestinian-American and German-American heritage, inside a United States in which the first Gulf War's televised aftermath and the rise of cable news had begun to render distant strangers' suffering into background ambient noise. The book it appeared in addressed a culture in which Arab-American voices had only just begun publishing love-and-care lyrics into mainstream American poetry, and in which ordinary domestic tenderness had quietly become a counter-public argument against a wider consumer indifference."

• Building sentence: "The piece appeared in her collection Red Suitcase (1994), composed in the years she had begun travelling between Texas, the West Bank, and Jordan as a working poet bridging Anglo-American and Arab-American traditions through children's-poetry editing, public-school residencies, and translation work. Behind the poem stood her father Aziz Shihab — a Palestinian refugee turned San Antonio journalist — and a Texan public sphere in which the father stepping into traffic with a child remained one of the rare daily images that could still draw a stranger's gaze from a screen."

• Thesis: "Across alert present-tense observation of the father stepping into traffic, an extended commercial-cargo metaphor whose absent FRAGILE label indicts the culture that labels packages but not parents, and a closing first-person-plural pivot that names the world's permanent rain and wide road, Nye stages the speaker's feelings about caring for someone vulnerable as an argument that ordinary tenderness is the precondition of communal survival."

• Topic: The opening stanzas establish the father's care through the watchfulness of his action, rendered in present-tense observation that places the reader at the kerb beside him.

• Technique: Present-tense free verse with negative commands that exclude rather than instruct ("No car must... No car drive too near").

• Evidence: "A man crosses the street in rain, / stepping gently, looking two times north and south, / because his son is asleep on his shoulder. / / No car must splash him. / No car drive too near to his shadow."

• Close analysis: "Two times north and south" converts a single observation into sustained watchfulness — care is the doubled looking; the negative commands ("No car must") register what care is asking of the world rather than what it asks of the carer.

• Effects: The reader is positioned as the father's witness, sharing his attention to the cars he is watching; the negative commands produce bodily empathy — the reader's body braces with his.

• Author's purpose: Nye may be using the present-tense observation to argue that care is not a feeling but a discipline performed in real time — the father's love is visible only in the doubled looking.

• +C: The counter-reading is that the father is alone in his watchfulness — the negative commands are addressed to a world that is not listening; the care is wholly his to perform, and the poem leaves the father carrying the sleeping son under the rain alone.

• Topic: The central stanzas turn from the father's watchfulness to the social context that fails to recognise it — the world has labels for fragile commercial goods but not for fragile human ones.

• Technique: Extended commercial-cargo metaphor paired with the absent label — capital-letter hypothetical text ("FRAGILE, HANDLE WITH CARE") that the jacket does NOT bear.

• Evidence: "This man carries the world's most sensitive cargo / but he's not marked. / Nowhere does his jacket say FRAGILE, / HANDLE WITH CARE."

• Close analysis: "World's most sensitive cargo" re-frames the child in the language of logistics — the rhetorical move sets up the absent label; the capitalised "FRAGILE, HANDLE WITH CARE" is type the jacket does not bear — its absence is the visual evidence the poem produces.

• Effects: The reader notices the missing label — what would be printed on a parcel is not printed on a parent's jacket; the effect is indignation produced by the visible absence of the type that should be there.

• Author's purpose: Nye appears to use the cargo metaphor to expose the moral inadequacy of commercial-care vocabulary — society protects packages but not people; the rhetorical move converts a description of one father into an indictment of a culture.

• +C: The image "His ear fills up with breathing" — the father hearing the son's dream "deep inside him" — admits the reading that the father has labels the world does not provide; care has its own language, intimate and unspoken, and the absence of the public label is the precondition of the private hearing.

• Topic: The closing stanzas convert the particular observation into a universal moral argument and a flat statement of the lasting conditions that make care necessary — and the conversion is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: First-person plural turn ("We're not going to be able to live") paired with two flat statements about the world's permanence.

• Evidence: "We're not going to be able to live in this world / if we're not willing to do what he's doing / with one another. / / The road will only be wide. / The rain will never stop falling."

• Close analysis: "With one another" converts the parent-child specifics into mutual obligation between any two persons; the future-tense "will only... will never" names the world's permanence — the rain and road will outlast every interaction, so the interaction must adjust.

• Effects: The reader experiences the moral pull-in — the "we" implicates the reader directly; the closing absolutes produce calm rather than panic, because the conditions are already known.

• Author's purpose: Nye may be arguing that care is not optional ornament on social life but the underlying condition of social life — without it, the wide road and the falling rain win.

• +C: The generative tension lies in the relationship between the universal "we" and the particular father — the universal claim is mounted on the back of one observation, and the leap from one to all is the poem's risk. The poem argues that the leap is justified because the conditions (rain, road, vulnerability) are universal even when the labels are not.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Nye deploys alert present-tense observation, an extended commercial-cargo metaphor exposing the absence of social care-labels, and a pivot from one particular father to a universal moral code to present caring for the vulnerable as the precondition of communal survival."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the conversion of one father's discipline into the underlying moral code by which any community survives the rain that will not stop.

In both 'Shoulders' and 'Choices', the speakers consider an act of care. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these acts of care? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — Choices (Tess Gallagher)

I go to the mountain side

of the house to cut saplings,

and clear a view to snow

on the mountain. But when I look up,

saw in hand, I see a nest

clutched in the uppermost branches.

I don't cut that one.

I don't cut the others either.

Suddenly, in every tree,

an unseen nest

where a mountain would be.

• Topic sentence: Nye presents care through third-person observational voice, the carer witnessed from outside and the speaker advocating for the carer's invisible discipline.

• Technique: Third-person present-tense observation fused with negative commands addressed to the world beyond the carer.

• Evidence: "A man crosses the street in rain, / stepping gently, looking two times north and south, / because his son is asleep on his shoulder. / / No car must splash him. / No car drive too near to his shadow."

• Close analysis: "Two times north and south" converts watchfulness into measured discipline; the negative commands "No car must" register what care is asking of the world rather than what care asks of the carer.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into the witness-position alongside the speaker, bodily bracing with the father.

• Author's purpose: Nye may be using observational distance to argue that care is a discipline performed in real time, visible only when an outside speaker translates it into public argument.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Nye presents care through third-person observation of an outside subject, Gallagher presents care through first-person discovery — the carer IS the speaker, and care is registered as the moment a plan changes.

• Technique: First-person past-tense narration of an interrupted action, fused with the change in seeing that spreads from the interruption.

• Evidence: "I don't cut that one. / I don't cut the others either. / Suddenly, in every tree, / an unseen nest / where a mountain would be."

• Close analysis: The repeated "I don't cut" converts the saw-stoppage into ethical decision; "Suddenly, in every tree" extends the discovery from one nest to all trees, the change in seeing arriving inside the speaker's eyes.

• Effects: The reader is positioned as the carer mid-decision rather than as the witness to one.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Gallagher may be using first-person interruption-grammar to argue that care re-orders private seeing the moment it is noticed once; the difference reveals that Nye's care must be argued for publicly, while Gallagher's care spreads privately the instant the nest is seen.
