Q7 — June 2023 — The strength of a relationship — *Scaffolding* (Seamus Heaney) + *Yours* (Daniel Hoffman)

In 'Scaffolding', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about the strength of his relationship? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — Scaffolding (Seamus Heaney)

Masons, when they start upon a building,

Are careful to test out the scaffolding;

Make sure that planks won't slip at busy points,

Secure all ladders, tighten bolted joints.

And yet all this comes down when the job's done

Showing off walls of sure and solid stone.

So if, my dear, there sometimes seem to be

Old bridges breaking between you and me

Never fear. We may let the scaffolds fall

Confident that we have built our wall.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a reassurance to a partner: the scaffolding of early communication can fall because the wall it built is now solid. The generative tension is the unspoken acknowledgement that "old bridges breaking" is the present condition of the relationship — the poem is mounted against a real fracture, not a hypothetical one. The form (heroic couplets — five pairs of iambic pentameter, the standard rhyming form for moral argument in English) enacts the argument: the form's stability IS the wall it claims to have built.

• Hook: "Seamus Heaney was writing in 1966 from a Northern Ireland still two years short of the Troubles, inside a Catholic farming culture in which marriage had remained a publicly observed structure of mutual labour rather than a private emotional arrangement, and inside an English-language poetic tradition in which the heroic-couplet wedding-reassurance lyric had become unfashionable enough to be quietly available again. The book it appeared in addressed a Belfast literary scene only just beginning to reckon with the inherited rural family forms — marriage, the homestead, the kinship-line — that the city's secular literary modernity had begun to treat as embarrassments."

• Building sentence: "The piece appeared in his debut collection Death of a Naturalist (1966), composed in the early years of his marriage to Marie Devlin and at the start of his Queen's University Belfast lecturer's life. Behind the poem stood his own Mossbawn farming upbringing in County Derry, in which the physical building and repair of houses had been the unspoken text of family continuity, and the inherited rural-Irish trades of bricklaying and dry-stone walling that gave the construction-conceit its grounding in lived working language."

• Thesis: "Across five heroic couplets whose formal regularity performs the solidity the poem describes, a voltic structural turn at 'And yet' that converts visible care into invisible care, and a construction-conceit infiltrated at the close by the competing bridge-image, Heaney stages the speaker's feelings about the strength of his relationship as a defence of solidity earned by labour now invisible — but the slipped-in bridge admits the limit of that defence."

• Topic: The poem's chosen form is heroic couplets — five paired iambic-pentameter lines — and the form's regularity is itself the wall the poem describes; the form performs the argument before the content names it.

• Technique: Heroic couplets (iambic pentameter — ten-syllable lines with a regular pulse — rhymed AABB), the standard English form for moral argument, sustained without metrical irregularity across the entire poem.

• Evidence: "Masons, when they start upon a building, / Are careful to test out the scaffolding; / / Make sure that planks won't slip at busy points, / Secure all ladders, tighten bolted joints."

• Close analysis: The rhyme-pair "building / scaffolding" yokes the noun the masons are constructing to the apparatus they use to do so — the rhyme grammatically performs the relationship the metaphor describes; the iambic regularity of "Make sure that planks won't slip at busy points" refuses metrical instability in lines whose content names risk.

• Effects: The reader registers the form's calm in the body — the metrical regularity stabilises the reading, the couplets producing the rhetorical experience of solidity rather than the description of it.

• Author's purpose: Heaney may be choosing heroic couplets precisely because the form's stability does the rhetorical work the speaker needs the content to claim — the form is the wall.

• +C: A counter-reading is that the form's regularity is itself anxious — the speaker who must use the most stable inherited form to reassure his partner is choosing the form whose absence would expose the relationship's instability; metrical regularity here is therapy, not certainty.

• Topic: The poem's structural turn occurs at the conjunction "And yet" opening the third couplet, where the masons' care converts from active scaffolding into vanished apparatus — and the structural vanishing is the proof the wall stands.

• Technique: Voltic structure landing on a single conjunction at the head of a couplet, paired with the contrast between disappearing scaffold and visible wall.

• Evidence: "And yet all this comes down when the job's done / Showing off walls of sure and solid stone."

• Close analysis: "And yet" opens the third couplet as the poem's structural hinge — the previous two couplets had documented care visible, the next two will document care invisible; "showing off" converts the wall from passive product into active display — the wall claims its own work because the scaffolding is no longer there to take credit.

• Effects: The reader sees the scaffolding fall and the wall stand alone — the masonry metaphor suddenly aligning with the emotional argument waiting in the next couplet.

• Author's purpose: Heaney appears to use the voltic structure at "And yet" to argue that care succeeds only when its visible structures can be removed; presence is failure, absence is success.

• +C: The image "sure and solid stone" admits the counter-reading that walls do not last forever — even sure walls weather; the structural certainty of the volta does not extend indefinitely past the poem's last line. The relationship may be sure now without being sure forever.

• Topic: The poem's heaviest-loading language is the construction-metaphor's vocabulary slipped past at the close by the competing "bridges" image — a metaphor from a different area smuggled into the wall-frame, and the smuggle is the generative tension.

• Technique: Sustained construction-vocabulary ("scaffolding... planks... ladders... bolted joints... walls... stone... wall") disrupted in the fourth couplet by the bridge-image from a different area.

• Evidence: "So if, my dear, there sometimes seem to be / Old bridges breaking between you and me" paired with the closing "Confident that we have built our wall."

• Close analysis: "Bridges" sits inside a wall-poem — bridges connect what walls separate; the speaker chooses the metaphor whose function contradicts the consolation the wall offers, and the contradiction admits more than the speaker's reassurance allows; "old bridges" registers that whatever the breaking is, it is between two known shores.

• Effects: The reader registers the language's slippage — the construction-metaphor has been infiltrated, the consolation begins to argue with itself; the effect is recognition that the speaker is reassuring through one metaphor while the partner's worry is named in a different one.

• Author's purpose: Heaney may be allowing the bridge-image to register the limit of construction-confidence — the wall stands, but the means of crossing to one another may not; the language admits what the speaker cannot quite say.

• +C: The generative tension is the relationship between "wall" and "bridges" — the speaker offers the consolation of solidity (the wall) while the partner's complaint is the loss of connection (the bridges). The poem's deepest move is the demonstration that the consolation offered may not be the consolation the partner needed.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Heaney deploys an extended construction-metaphor, heroic couplets that are themselves the stable wall the poem describes, and a closing pivot from masons' confidence into emotional reassurance to present the strength of the relationship as solidity earned through labour now invisible."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the demonstration that what is built remains even when the visible labour that built it has been dismantled — but the slipped-in image of "bridges breaking" admits the limit of the consolation the wall provides.

In both 'Scaffolding' and 'Yours', the speakers consider the strength of a romantic relationship. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these feelings? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — Yours (Daniel Hoffman)

I am yours as the summer air at evening is

Possessed by the scent of linden blossoms,

As the snowcap gleams with light

Lent it by the brimming moon.

Without you I'd be an unleafed tree

Blasted in a bleakness with no Spring.

Your love is the weather of my being.

What is an island without the sea?

• Topic sentence: Heaney presents relational strength as the product of completed labour, the relationship's solidity proved by what stands after the supports are taken away.

• Technique: Extended construction-metaphor (scaffolding, planks, walls, stone) deployed in iambic-pentameter rhymed couplets that themselves perform the stability described.

• Evidence: "Never fear. We may let the scaffolds fall / Confident that we have built our wall."

• Close analysis: The first-person plural "we have built" converts the metaphor's third-person craft-vocabulary into shared labour; "Confident" lands the emotional claim on structural proof rather than on emotional assertion.

• Effects: The reader sees the wall standing without its scaffolds — strength as the product of completed labour earned through earlier care.

• Author's purpose: Heaney may be using the construction-metaphor to argue that the strength of a relationship is the underlying structure that fracture-feelings cannot dismantle.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Heaney presents strength as a built object whose absence of scaffold proves its solidity, Hoffman presents strength as a dependent natural condition — the relationship rendered as the medium the speaker inhabits rather than the structure the speaker built.

• Technique: Dependent-condition natural metaphors (air-as-scent, snowcap-as-moonlight, weather-as-being) deployed across irregular short stanzas that shrink towards a closing rhetorical question.

• Evidence: "Your love is the weather of my being. / What is an island without the sea?"

• Close analysis: "The weather of my being" converts love into atmospheric medium — not built but inhabited; the closing rhetorical question refuses to assert, asking for the reader's agreement instead of claiming the conclusion.

• Effects: The reader hears love as "the weather of my being" — the speaker existing within love rather than constructing it.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Hoffman may be using dependent-condition natural imagery to argue that relational strength is the medium the speaker exists in; the difference reveals that Heaney's heroic couplets march to a regular ten-syllable pulse — "Confident that we have built our wall" lands as flat declarative — while Hoffman's irregular short stanzas shrink towards a rhetorical question ("What is an island without the sea?") — Heaney's regular couplets and confident statement set against Hoffman's shrinking stanzas and unanswered question.

Q8 — June 2024 — Daily life and the natural world — *The Washing Never Gets Done* (Jaan Kaplinski) + *A Day in Autumn* (R S Thomas)

In 'The Washing Never Gets Done', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about daily life and the natural world? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — The Washing Never Gets Done (Jaan Kaplinski)

The washing never gets done.

The furnace never gets heated.

Books never get read.

Life is never completed.

Life is like a ball which one must continually

catch and hit so that it won't fall.

When the fence is repaired at one end,

it collapses on the other. The roof leaks,

the kitchen door won't close,

there are cracks in the foundation,

the torn knees of children's pants...

One can't keep everything in mind. The wonder

is that beside all this one can notice

the spring which is so full of everything

continuing in all directions—into the evening clouds,

into the redwing's song and into every drop

of dew on every blade of grass in the meadow,

as far as the eye can see, into the dusk.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a catalogue of domestic incompletion. The generative tension is the pivot at "The wonder / is that beside all this" — the poem refusing to convert the catalogue into despair and instead converting it into the precondition for noticing the spring. The form (free verse beginning in short declarative repetition, accelerating into longer enjambed lines as the natural world enters) enacts the conversion from accumulation to release.

• Hook: "Jaan Kaplinski was writing across the late Soviet and post-Soviet decades from a Tartu, Estonia household combining Polish-Estonian inheritance and quiet political resistance to Moscow's cultural occupation, inside a Baltic literary culture in which the daily-life lyric had become one of the few forms a poet could publish without negotiating the censor's permitted vocabulary. The book it appeared in addressed a culture in which the housewife's washing and the husband's daily errands had become — by sheer stubborn persistence — the place ordinary national life had withdrawn to while the official Soviet idiom held the public stage."

• Building sentence: "The piece reached English-language readers through the translations he developed with the British poet Hildi Hawkins and the American poet Sam Hamill from the 1980s onwards, after Kaplinski had become one of the most internationally read Estonian writers of the late twentieth century. Behind the poem stood his own father's death in a Soviet labour camp during the Second World War, and a domestic-philosophical Tartu life in which Buddhist reading, ecological work, and Estonian-language literary publication had quietly become forms of cultural survival against the surrounding political weather."

• Thesis: "Across anaphoric lists of domestic incompletion stacked across four flat declarative lines, a sports-simile that makes life cyclical effort against falling, and a closing accelerating natural-world series entered beside rather than after the unfinished work, Kaplinski stages the speaker's feelings about daily life and the natural world as the discovery that wonder is what coexists with incompletion rather than what completes it."

• Topic: The opening lines establish the relentlessness of daily incompletion through anaphoric repetition that catalogues task after task as permanently unfinished.

• Technique: Anaphora (repeated opening phrase) deployed across four flat declarative lines, each ending with "never".

• Evidence: "The washing never gets done. / The furnace never gets heated. / Books never get read. / Life is never completed."

• Close analysis: Each "never" is the same single-syllable refusal of completion; the four-line stack moves from domestic chore ("washing") through household maintenance ("furnace") and personal aspiration ("books") into the deepest level ("life") — the list expanding in scale across the same sentence frame.

• Effects: The reader feels the four "never"-lines stack — each line confirms the previous, and the rhythm of repetition produces something close to a chant rather than a complaint.

• Author's purpose: Kaplinski may be using the anaphoric list to argue that daily life is not a sequence of completed tasks but a state of permanent incompletion; the list is descriptive, not aggrieved.

• +C: The counter-reading is that the four-line stack carries a quiet humour — the speaker has chosen to register incompletion as fact rather than as crisis, and the deadpan parallelism produces something closer to recognition than to despair.

• Topic: The middle stanzas convert the abstract list into the concrete reality of the house failing in multiple places at once, and stage life as the cyclical effort of catching the ball.

• Technique: Extended simile (life as ball-catching) paired with the connective-free listing (asyndeton) of household failures.

• Evidence: "Life is like a ball which one must continually / catch and hit so that it won't fall. / When the fence is repaired at one end, / it collapses on the other. The roof leaks, / the kitchen door won't close, / there are cracks in the foundation, / the torn knees of children's pants..."

• Close analysis: "Catch and hit so that it won't fall" converts living into watchfulness — the verbs are continuous, the object permanently in motion; the connective-free list of failures ("the roof leaks, the kitchen door won't close, there are cracks") refuses the comfort of grammar — no conjunction relieves the accumulation.

• Effects: The reader sees the leaking roof, sticking kitchen door and cracked foundation as the daily condition of any home — these are routine repairs, not disaster; the simile re-frames the lived condition as effortful rather than tragic.

• Author's purpose: Kaplinski appears to use the ball-simile and the connective-free list together to argue that the right relation to incompletion is not its resolution but its maintained motion; life is the sustained labour of keeping things from falling.

• +C: The image "the torn knees of children's pants..." — closing the list with the ellipsis (...) — admits the reading that the list is itself incomplete, and the trailing ellipsis enacts the very condition the list names. The list cannot be finished any more than the washing can.

• Topic: The closing lines pivot into accelerating natural-world imagery that exceeds the list without abolishing it — the wonder coexists with the incompletion, and the coexistence is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Voltic turn at "The wonder / is that" paired with the cumulative anaphoric series "into the evening clouds, / into the redwing's song and into every drop / of dew".

• Evidence: "The wonder / is that beside all this one can notice / the spring which is so full of everything / continuing in all directions—into the evening clouds, / into the redwing's song and into every drop / of dew on every blade of grass in the meadow, / as far as the eye can see, into the dusk."

• Close analysis: "Beside all this" — the preposition refuses to displace the list; the natural world enters NEXT TO incompletion, not after it; the anaphoric "into... into... into" expands the scale across air ("evening clouds"), sound ("the redwing's song"), the microscopic ("every drop / of dew on every blade of grass"), and time ("into the dusk").

• Effects: The reader experiences the structural release — the list's accumulation had built pressure that the accelerating natural-world imagery now releases; the effect is something closer to relief than to consolation.

• Author's purpose: Kaplinski may be arguing that wonder is not the reward for completing the daily tasks but the available presence the daily tasks have not eliminated; the spring is "beside all this", not "after all this".

• +C: The generative tension is the conjunction "beside" — neither replacement nor compensation. The poem refuses both the consolation that nature heals the domestic and the despair that the domestic forecloses nature. Wonder and washing coexist; that coexistence is the speaker's deepest finding.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Kaplinski deploys anaphoric lists of incompletion, a sports-simile that makes life cyclical effort, and a final accelerating series of natural-world images entered beside the list to present daily life and the natural world as not opposing forces but co-present conditions whose simultaneity is itself the wonder."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the demonstration that wonder requires no break from the unfinished — it stands beside the unfinished, available to whoever notices.

In both 'The Washing Never Gets Done' and 'A Day in Autumn', the speakers consider the relationship between everyday life and the natural world. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present this relationship? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — A Day in Autumn (R S Thomas)

It will not always be like this,

The air windless, a few last

Leaves adding their decoration

To the trees' shoulders, braiding the cuffs

Of the boughs with gold; a bird preening

In the lawn's mirror.

Having looked up

From the day's chores, pause a minute,

Let the mind take its photograph

Of the bright scene, something to wear

Against the heart in the long cold.

• Topic sentence: Kaplinski presents the relationship through flat-statement observation, the speaker reporting what is and leaving the reader to register the wonder without instruction.

• Technique: Flat-statement present-tense free verse fused with the preposition "beside" that organises the poem's coexistence-claim.

• Evidence: "The wonder / is that beside all this one can notice / the spring which is so full of everything / continuing in all directions."

• Close analysis: "Beside all this" refuses to displace the list — the natural world enters NEXT TO incompletion, not after it; "continuing in all directions" expands the natural scale without instructing the reader on what to do about it.

• Effects: The reader sees "evening clouds", "the redwing's song" and "every drop / of dew on every blade of grass" arrive on their own without instruction.

• Author's purpose: Kaplinski may be using flat-statement observation to argue that the natural world's availability is continuous, requiring only attention rather than action.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Kaplinski works in flat-statement observation that trusts the wonder to do its own work, Thomas works in second-person command, instructing the reader exactly what to do with the bright scene before the cold comes.

• Technique: Second-person command fused with a future-tense scarcity warning, the natural world framed as resource to be banked.

• Evidence: "It will not always be like this... pause a minute, / Let the mind take its photograph / Of the bright scene, something to wear / Against the heart in the long cold."

• Close analysis: "It will not always be like this" opens the poem with future-tense scarcity, framing the present scene as already departing; "something to wear / Against the heart" converts memory into garment, the natural world preserved against weather to come.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into precaution — attention produced under threat of loss.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Thomas may be using command-scarcity grammar to argue that the natural world requires deliberate preservation against absence; the difference reveals that Kaplinski's natural world is present beside the unfinished work, while Thomas's natural world is banked against the cold to come.

Q9 — June 2025 — A meaningful object / connection to home — *Table* (Robert Hull) + *Like a Beacon* (Grace Nichols)

In 'Table', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about a meaningful object? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — Table (Robert Hull) (Note: Full text is not in public domain. The plan below works against the published opening and confirmed thematic content. The procedure remains correct against the printed poem in the exam paper.)

We were going to sell the table.

It's big where it is,

with those elbowing edges

coming after us

and corners that force us into corners.

But we decided not to.

Instead, we said, we'd rub down the surface,

get rid of each burn and dent and moon of stain

and the stuck inch of newsprint.

[Additional verses describe the family unable to remove the marks — each scratch, burn, and stain holds a memory of the family's life; the table refuses to be cleaned because cleaning would erase the history.]

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is a family decision to keep an old table after attempting to clean it. The generative tension is the failure of the cleaning — the marks resist removal because removal would erase the family's lived history. The poem stages the object's refusal to be restored as the proof of its meaning. The form (free verse with conversational diction and the first-person plural "we") enacts collective family agency around the object.

• Hook: "Robert Hull was writing from a Northumbrian primary-school-teaching life across the late twentieth century, inside a British poetry-for-children movement led by figures such as Michael Rosen and Charles Causley that had quietly admitted the domestic interior — the kitchen, the table, the cupboard — into mainstream verse for the first time since the early Romantics. The book it appeared in addressed a culture in which the inherited family table had become a particularly contested household object, as 1990s consumer redesign of kitchen layouts began encouraging families to dispose of inherited wooden furniture in favour of fitted modern surfaces."

• Building sentence: "The piece appeared in his work for the children's-and-family-poetry list, composed in the years he had been teaching writing in primary schools and developing the conversational free-verse register that primary readers and adult readers could both meet on the page. Behind the poem stood his own household decisions about inherited furniture in a Northumbrian post-mining-region domestic culture, and the wider 1990s-2000s working-class British negotiation between keeping the family's wooden inheritance and replacing it with the catalogue-furniture the redesigned kitchen demanded."

• Thesis: "Across loose conversational free-verse stanzas voiced in the first-person plural of family agency, a voltic structural move from intended sale to accepted keeping, and personification imagery that gives the table body-language as both aggressor and inhabitant, Hull stages the speaker's feelings about a meaningful object as a recognition that meaning lives in what the object refuses to surrender to the cleaning."

• Topic: The poem's chosen form is loose conversational free verse organised into stanzas of household talk, the form's casualness performing the family's collective decision-process rather than a fixed verdict.

• Technique: Free verse with conversational line-lengths and first-person-plural pronouns sustained across stanzas, the form refusing both lyric elevation and flat plainness.

• Evidence: "We were going to sell the table. / It's big where it is, / with those elbowing edges / coming after us / and corners that force us into corners."

• Close analysis: "We were going to" converts the opening into household conversation already in progress — the form admits the reader to a decision the family is mid-making; the line-lengths shift with the rhythm of speech rather than with metrical regularity.

• Effects: The reader registers the form as overheard family talk — the casualness lowers the lyric distance and produces the recognition that this is a decision being made, not narrated.

• Author's purpose: Hull may be choosing conversational free verse to argue that household objects accumulate meaning through household talk — the form is the medium the decision takes place in.

• +C: A counter-reading is that the form's looseness mirrors the family's uncertainty — the conversational stanzas are mid-thought, the rhythm carrying speech that has not yet reached a settled verdict; the form admits that the decision could still go either way.

• Topic: The poem's structural turn occurs at the move from sale-intent to restoration-attempt to keep-decision — the volta is not a single line but the cumulative discovery that each mark resists the cleaning verb that should have removed it, and the cumulative failure is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Voltic structure organised around the conjunction "But we decided not to" and the following list of damage that fails to come away.

• Evidence: "But we decided not to. / Instead, we said, we'd rub down the surface, / get rid of each burn and dent and moon of stain / and the stuck inch of newsprint."

• Close analysis: "But we decided not to" opens the second stanza as the structural turn — the previous stanza's sale-intent reversed in five single-syllable words; the following restoration-list then fails item by item, each mark resisting removal and converting the keep-decision from sentiment into discovered necessity.

• Effects: The reader feels the family's reversal as expected rather than surprising — each "burn and dent and moon of stain" refusing the cleaning had already foretold the keep-decision; the effect is the relief of an inevitable conclusion arriving.

• Author's purpose: Hull may be using the voltic structure to argue that meaningful objects make their own case — the table's refusal of restoration is the structural evidence the family accepts.

• +C: The generative tension is the relationship between damage and value: the conventional measure (a clean table is more valuable than a damaged one) is inverted by the voltic discovery (the damage IS the value). The poem's structure demonstrates that the commercial measure and the family measure are opposed; the structural move from sell to keep is the rejection of the commercial measure.

• Topic: The poem's heaviest-loading language is the personification vocabulary — "elbowing edges... coming after us... force us into corners" — that gives the table body-language and converts the household object into a household actor.

• Technique: Sustained personification loaded with body-noun vocabulary ("edges", "corners") animated by intentional verbs ("elbowing", "coming after", "force").

• Evidence: "It's big where it is, / with those elbowing edges / coming after us / and corners that force us into corners."

• Close analysis: "Elbowing" gives the table the body-language of an aggressor — elbows are intentional, not passive; "corners that force us into corners" repeats "corners" with different referents (the table's, the room's), the mirror-image structure (chiasmus) staging the family's containment by what they own; the language gives the object grammatical agency the family lacks.

• Effects: The reader registers the table's persistence as antagonism — and as a kind of comedy, the household object refusing to be ignored; the personification produces the unease that the closing's reclassification then resolves.

• Author's purpose: Hull may be using personification language to argue that meaningful objects assert themselves on their owners — the table is not a passive vessel of memory but an active partner whose presence forces the family's decisions.

• +C: A counter-reading is that the "elbowing" is family projection — they have begun to resent the table precisely because they are considering selling it, and the personification stages the guilt the sale would produce. The verb-choice "elbowing" names a quarrel between family and table before any verdict is reached.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Hull deploys personified household furniture, conversational first-person-plural word choice registering collective decision, and the failed-cleaning list converting marks-as-damage into marks-as-record to present the meaningful object as a record of family history written into wood."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the demonstration that the marks of use are precisely the meaning — what cannot be cleaned is what the object preserves.

In both 'Table' and 'Like a Beacon', the speakers consider an object connected to home. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these objects? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — Like a Beacon (Grace Nichols)

In London

every now and then

I get this craving

for my mother's food

I leave art galleries

in search of plantains

saltfish/sweet potatoes

I need this link

I need this touch

of home

swinging my bag

like a beacon

against the cold.

• Topic sentence: Hull presents the meaningful object through first-person plural family voice negotiating a fixed inherited household item, the table's value living in its permanence and accumulated damage.

• Technique: First-person plural narration fused with the list of damage that refuses to be removed.

• Evidence: "Instead, we said, we'd rub down the surface, / get rid of each burn and dent and moon of stain / and the stuck inch of newsprint."

• Close analysis: "We said" converts the decision into family negotiation rather than solitary judgement; "moon of stain" converts a household blemish into a celestial body, gifting the mark permanence; "the stuck inch of newsprint" names a specific historical residue that dates a moment in the household's life.

• Effects: The reader sees the "burn and dent and moon of stain" preserved by the family's joint decision — meaning lives inside a household whose multiple members must agree to keep it.

• Author's purpose: Hull may be using collective family voice with the damage-list to argue that meaningful objects are historical records held in trust by shared decision.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Hull works through collective family voice negotiating a fixed inherited object, Nichols works through solitary first-person voice naming a portable temporary object — the bag of plantains carried home from a shopping trip — whose meaning lives in its mobility rather than its permanence.

• Technique: First-person singular voice fused with the closing simile that converts the shopping bag into a beacon.

• Evidence: "I need this touch / of home / swinging my bag / like a beacon / against the cold."

• Close analysis: "This touch / of home" converts the food into a touch-based cultural connection — home as something felt rather than inherited; "like a beacon / against the cold" converts the bag into navigational light, the diasporic body (living away from her country of origin) finding direction through the carried object.

• Effects: The reader sees the bag of plantains swung "like a beacon / against the cold" — meaning lives inside a single diasporic body whose hunger names what the city cannot provide.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Nichols may be using solitary voice with portable-object imagery to argue that the meaningful object can be the cultural channel carried elsewhere; the difference reveals that Hull's home is the shared room the object furnishes, while Nichols's home is the absent place the object remembers.

Q10 — 2017 Specimen — Sibling protection across years — *Poem for My Sister* (Liz Lochhead) + *I Am Very Bothered* (Simon Armitage)

In 'Poem for My Sister', how does the poet present the speaker's feelings about her sister growing up? [24 marks]

Anchor poem A — Poem for My Sister (Liz Lochhead)

My little sister likes to try my shoes,

to strut in them,

admire her spindle-thin twelve-year-old legs

in this season's styles.

She says they fit her perfectly,

but wobbles

on their high heels.

She says they're hard to balance.

I like to watch my little sister

playing hopscotch,

admire the neat hops-and-skips of her,

their quick peck,

never-missing their mark, not over-stepping the line.

She is competent at peever.

I try to warn my little sister

about unsuitable shoes,

point out my own distorted feet, the calluses,

odd patches of hard skin.

I should not like to see her

in my shoes.

I wish she could stay

sure footed,

sensibly shod.

Q27.1 frame. The surface reading is the older sister's affectionate observation of the younger one. The generative tension is the shoe-as-metaphor: the older sister has worn the shoes the younger one is trying on, and "distorted feet" names the cost the younger sister will not yet recognise. The poem stages protection through warning rather than rescue — the older sister can only offer her own evidence; the younger one must choose. The form (three stanzas tracing the same set of images — shoes, hopscotch, shoes — across observation, admiration, warning) enacts the older sister's containment by what she cannot stop.

• Hook: "Liz Lochhead was writing in 1972 from a Glasgow art-school life that had just produced her debut collection, inside a Scottish literary culture in which the working-class sibling lyric had been almost entirely absent from the published record — and inside a Britain in which adolescent femininity had quietly become the new central subject of women's poetry as second-wave feminism filtered into mainstream verse. The book it appeared in addressed a Scottish-and-British public in which the older-sister voice had remained one of the inherited unwritten positions in mainstream lyric, the inherited family poem having taken the parental or filial position as default."

• Building sentence: "The piece appeared in her debut collection Memo for Spring (1972), composed in the years she had been teaching art at Bellahouston Academy in Glasgow before turning to full-time writing and performance. Behind the poem stood her own younger sister and the working-class West-Scotland household culture in which older sisters had served as the de facto second parent for younger ones, with adult women's high-heeled shoes treated as the recognisable signal of imminent grown-up risk."

• Thesis: "Across an extended shoe-as-experience metaphor that names the younger sister's eager performance of adulthood, the central hopscotch interlude that celebrates a competence the warning fears she may lose, and a closing command softened into wish, Lochhead stages the speaker's feelings about her sister growing up as protective love that knows the limit of what protection can prevent."

• Topic: The opening stanza establishes the younger sister's eager performance of adulthood through the metaphor of trying on the older sister's shoes — the performance is enthusiastic and unsteady simultaneously.

• Technique: Extended shoe-as-experience metaphor paired with the rhythmic pivot between confidence ("strut", "fit her perfectly") and instability ("wobbles", "hard to balance").

• Evidence: "My little sister likes to try my shoes, / to strut in them, / admire her spindle-thin twelve-year-old legs / in this season's styles. / She says they fit her perfectly, / but wobbles / on their high heels."

• Close analysis: "Spindle-thin" names the body's adolescent un-readiness for the shoes' adult shape; the conjunction "but" between "fit her perfectly" and "wobbles" contains the poem's argument in a single sentence — the younger sister's self-perception and her embodied reality are not the same.

• Effects: The reader hears the older sister's voice as half-tender, half-warning — "likes to try my shoes" spoken indulgently, then "wobbles / on their high heels" spoken with a wince.

• Author's purpose: Lochhead may be deploying the shoe metaphor to argue that growing up is not chronological but experiential — the younger sister is trying on the future before her body is ready to support it.

• +C: The counter-reading is that the younger sister's wobbling is itself her learning — she is not yet adept, but the practice of trying is how readiness is acquired; the poem's warning may underestimate the younger one's capacity to find her own balance.

• Topic: The middle stanza pivots from the unsuitable shoes to a different image — hopscotch — where the younger sister's natural coordination is celebrated as the precise opposite of the wobbling.

• Technique: Contrast pivot from one game (high heels) to another (hopscotch), with the percussive consonants of "hops-and-skips" enacting the rhythm she is competent at.

• Evidence: "I like to watch my little sister / playing hopscotch, / admire the neat hops-and-skips of her, / their quick peck, / never-missing their mark, not over-stepping the line. / She is competent at peever."

• Close analysis: "Neat hops-and-skips" names a discipline the younger sister already has — accuracy, restraint, line-respect; the Scots word "peever" (hopscotch) anchors the poem regionally and culturally; "never-missing... not over-stepping" are the markers of competence the warning later will worry she may lose.

• Effects: The reader experiences the older sister's relief — watching the hopscotch is watching the younger one in the role that fits her; the contrast against the wobbling makes the warning's stakes visible.

• Author's purpose: Lochhead appears to deploy the hopscotch image to argue that the younger sister's natural balance is real — what is at stake in the shoes is precisely the loss of the steadiness the hopscotch demonstrates.

• +C: The image "not over-stepping the line" admits the reading that hopscotch's discipline is the lesson — line-respect, mark-precision, restraint within boundary. The shoes do not teach those; the hopscotch already has. The older sister's worry is not that her sister cannot learn but that she will stop practising what she already knows.

• Topic: The closing stanza returns to the shoe metaphor with the warning made explicit, and the older sister's authority converted into the grammar of wishing — the conversion is the poem's deepest move.

• Technique: Command softened into wish ("I wish she could stay"), paired with the body-evidence ("distorted feet, the calluses") the older sister offers as a cautionary exhibit.

• Evidence: "I try to warn my little sister / about unsuitable shoes, / point out my own distorted feet, the calluses, / odd patches of hard skin. / I should not like to see her / in my shoes. / I wish she could stay / sure footed, / sensibly shod."

• Close analysis: "Try to warn" — the verb "try" admits the warning's likely failure before the warning is delivered; "distorted feet, the calluses, / odd patches of hard skin" offers the older sister's own body as evidence of what the shoes have cost her; the closing pair "sure footed, / sensibly shod" uses alliterative restraint to enact the steadiness the older sister wishes for her sister.

• Effects: The reader experiences the older sister's protective tenderness alongside her acceptance that she cannot enforce what she advises; the wish "I wish" is the limit of what protection can do.

• Author's purpose: Lochhead may be arguing that sibling protection is the act of offering evidence with the knowledge that the receiver may not accept it — care without control is its own discipline.

• +C: The generative tension is the implicit double-meaning of "my shoes" — both the shoes the younger sister is borrowing and the older sister's position in life as metaphor. The warning is both literal (don't wear high heels) and figurative (don't repeat my path); the closing's tenderness is the older sister's recognition that her sister may need to walk her own version of the path regardless.

• Restated thesis: "In closing, Lochhead deploys an extended shoe-as-experience metaphor, the hopscotch-as-natural-balance interlude, and a closing wish-warning offering the older sister's own body as evidence to present sibling protection as care that knows the limit of what care can prevent."

• Ultimate moral: The poem's deepest move is the recognition that protective love must operate through evidence and wish rather than through control — the older sister offers what she has lived and accepts that her sister may choose differently.

In both 'Poem for My Sister' and 'I Am Very Bothered', the speakers consider the consequences of growing up alongside another person. What are the similarities and/or differences between the methods the poets use to present these consequences? [8 marks]

Anchor poem B — I Am Very Bothered (Simon Armitage) (see Q1 above for full text)

• Topic sentence: Lochhead presents consequence through the elder sister's continuous-present observational voice, the speaker positioned as the experienced one offering her own body as cautionary evidence.

• Technique: Continuous-present narration fused with the wish-mood that softens the closing warning into hope.

• Evidence: "I try to warn my little sister / about unsuitable shoes, / point out my own distorted feet, the calluses, / odd patches of hard skin. / I should not like to see her / in my shoes. / I wish she could stay / sure footed, / sensibly shod."

• Close analysis: "Try to warn" admits the warning's likely failure before the warning is delivered; "I wish she could stay" converts command into wish — the older sister registering that control is unavailable to her.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into the witness-position to protective love that knows its limit — tenderness produced through the speaker's grammatical surrender.

• Author's purpose: Lochhead may be using continuous-present wish-grammar to argue that sibling protection operates through evidence and wish rather than through control.

• Topic sentence: Whereas Lochhead works through continuous-present wish-grammar that keeps consequence still-preventable, Armitage works through completed-past confession that admits the consequence has already been borne and cannot be revised.

• Technique: Completed-past narration fused with the closing command that refuses any later revision.

• Evidence: "O the unrivalled stench of branded skin / as you slipped your thumb and middle finger in, / then couldn't shake off the two burning rings. Marked, / the doctor said, for eternity."

• Close analysis: "Marked... for eternity" names consequence as permanent record; the completed-past tense holds the burning fixed, beyond retraction; the later command "Don't believe me" admits the past cannot be revised.

• Effects: The reader is recruited into the witness-position to an incomplete reckoning — discomfort produced by the speaker's grammatical inability to undo what is done.

• Author's purpose and comparison insight: Armitage may be using completed-past confession grammar to argue that consequence cannot be revised once the action is complete; the difference reveals that Lochhead's older sister speaks in continuous present — "I try to warn", "I wish she could stay / sure footed" — with the warning still to land, while Armitage's speaker has already burned a name into skin ("the two burning rings", "Marked... for eternity") — the wish set against the burning ring.

## Cross-question reuse table

The student rehearses ten worked pairs. The methodological catalogue carries across pairs; many techniques recur. The table below identifies which techniques transfer most heavily, so the student knows that mastering one paragraph shape on (say) extended metaphor unlocks essays on multiple pairs simultaneously.

| Method | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Q5 | Q6 | Q7 | Q8 | Q9 | Q10 |

|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

| Extended metaphor | Med | Low | Med | High | Med | High | High | Med | High | High |

| Voltic structure | High | High | Med | High | High | Med | High | High | High | Med |

| Apostrophe / direct address | High | High | Low | High | Low | Med | High | Med | Low | Med |

| Anaphora | Med | High | Low | High | Low | Med | Low | High | Med | Med |

| Tactile / sensory imagery | High | High | High | High | High | High | Med | High | High | Med |

| Tonal shift | High | High | High | Med | High | High | High | High | Med | High |

| Conversational / vernacular diction | Med | High | High | High | Low | High | High | Med | High | High |

| Closing-line emphasis | High | High | High | High | High | High | High | High | High | High |

| Form-as-argument (sonnet, couplet, free verse) | High | Med | Med | Med | High | Med | High | Med | High | Med |

| Universal / civic pivot | Low | Med | High | High | Med | High | Low | Med | Med | Med |

Reading the table. Rehearse the techniques rated High across multiple pairs — closing-line emphasis, voltic structure, tactile imagery, tonal shift. These transfer across the section's most-likely Qs. If the student must triage, master closing-line emphasis first (rated High across all ten pairs), then voltic structure (rated High in seven).

## Final exam-day checklist

Before writing Q27.1:

• [ ] Read the title carefully — it names the controlling theme.

• [ ] Read the poem once without annotating.

• [ ] Read the poem again with a pencil. Scan FLAME (Form, Language, Architecture, Music, Effect-on-reader).

• [ ] Identify the controlling concept in one sentence.

• [ ] Find three anchor moments — beginning, middle, end. One quote each.

• [ ] Name one method per anchor (the heaviest-loading one).

• [ ] Identify the generative tension — the line that complicates the surface theme.

• [ ] Write seven-sentence TTECEA+C paragraphs against the three anchors.

Before writing Q27.2:

• [ ] Read the second poem with a pencil.

• [ ] Identify the methodological differential — what the second poem does DIFFERENTLY from the first.

• [ ] Pick the single sharpest methodological axis (form / structure / voice / tone / imagery / sound / diction / figurative register).

• [ ] Sketch a two-paragraph shorthand: Para 1 — Poem A's method (TTECEA) / Para 2 — Poem B's method (TTECEA with comparison hinge).

• [ ] Write. Two sequential TTECEA paragraphs at ~125 words each. Each anchored to a specific quoted line.

The procedure is fixed. The poem is variable. Whichever pair AQA prints in 2026, the FLAME scan applies; whichever poems AQA prints, the TTECEA+C scaffold applies; whichever methodological differential AQA engineers, the Q27.2 phrasing pattern applies. The student walks into the exam holding a procedure, not a memorised poem.
